summary: O'Neil argues that New York's Value-Added Model (VAM) for teacher evaluations functions essentially as a random number generator that arbitrarily determines which teachers receive tenure. Under Cuomo's new rules, teachers cannot receive 'effective' ratings if their VAM score is 'ineffective,' regardless of principal evaluations or classroom observations. Using probability calculations, she demonstrates that if 30% of teachers are deemed 'ineffective' by VAM in any given year, then 35% of teachers would be denied tenure over four years purely by chance. She contends this arbitrary punishment is the point—reformers prefer random evaluation to systems where most teachers pass.
key_points: VAM scores override all other evaluation methods: a teacher cannot receive an 'effective' rating regardless of principal reviews or observations if their VAM score is 'ineffective'
Using binomial probability, if 30% are deemed ineffective yearly, 35% of teachers would fail to achieve tenure over four years through pure randomness
The political utility of VAM lies precisely in its ability to arbitrarily punish teachers, wielding power over unions
Reformers explicitly prefer arbitrary evaluation systems to ones where most teachers are rated effective
The true 'ineffective' cutoff percentage is unknown but deliberately set high enough to fail a significant number of teachers
money_quotes: It is approximately a random number generator.
Even so, it's still in use, mostly because it wields power over the teacher unions.
This is the political power of a terrible scoring system.
More than a third of teachers are being arbitrarily chosen to be punished by this opaque and unaccountable test.
Think of it as a biased coin flip, and 30% of the time – for any teacher and for any year – it lands on 'ineffective'.
In fact, they'd rather see arbitrary and random evaluation than see most people get through unscathed.
key_insight: The VAM teacher evaluation system's value to reformers lies not in its accuracy but in its capacity to arbitrarily deny tenure to a substantial percentage of teachers, functioning as a tool of political power rather than genuine assessment.
